task: Log-likelihood keyword extraction (node vs. reference corpus, explicit LL threshold and minimum frequency cut-off)
notable human research: Dunning, T. (1993). Accurate methods for the statistics of surprise and coincidence. Computational Linguistics, 19(1), 61–74.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: LL keyword extraction is routine for trained corpus linguists using standard software.
Research Significance — Explanation: Keyword analysis is foundational to quantitative corpus linguistics methodology.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Modern AI can reliably script and execute LL extraction with explicit thresholds.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Within ten years this deterministic workflow will be trivially automated.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: A purely arithmetic algorithm presents no theoretical barrier to full automation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The LL threshold and frequency cut-off are easily formalised from methods prose.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Parameters like threshold values and minimum frequencies are routinely reported explicitly.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Identical corpora and thresholds produce bit-identical LL keyword lists every time.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: AntConc, #LancsBox, CQPweb, and quanteda all implement LL keyness end-to-end.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Almost no qualitative judgement is needed once parameters are fixed.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published keyword tables provide direct objective ground truth for replication.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Thresholds and reference corpora can be swept programmatically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Auditing reduces to spot-checking a handful of values against the published table.

task: BIC-based keyword ranking (BIC as association measure)
notable human research: Wilson, A. (2013). Embracing Bayes factors for key item analysis in corpus linguistics. In M. Bieswanger & A. Koll-Stobbe (Eds.), New approaches to the study of linguistic variability. Peter Lang.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: BIC keyword ranking is feasible for humans with statistical competence and scripting.
Research Significance — Explanation: BIC keyness offers a useful probabilistic alternative but is less central than LL.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can implement the BIC formula directly with appropriate guidance.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future systems will trivially automate the full BIC pipeline without oversight.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The BIC calculation is wholly mathematical and theoretically fully replaceable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Wilson's published formulae are explicit and easily parsed into executable code.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: BIC studies sometimes omit priors or comparison details needed for exact replication.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Deterministic given identical token counts and formula specification.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Fewer turnkey tools than LL but quanteda and custom R scripts provide coverage.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Minimal interpretation is needed beyond applying the ranking threshold.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published BIC rankings provide direct objective comparators.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: BIC thresholds and corpus partitions can be varied systematically and easily.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification reduces to recomputing BIC for a sample of top-ranked items.

task: Multi-word keyword / n-gram keyness (bigrams or trigrams)
notable human research: O'Donnell, M. B. (2011). The adjusted frequency list: A method to produce cluster-sensitive frequency lists. ICAME Journal, 35, 135–169.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: N-gram keyness extraction is standard practice using accessible corpus software.
Research Significance — Explanation: Multi-word keyness captures important phraseological patterning beyond single words.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can extract and score n-grams reliably with standard NLP tooling.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should automate n-gram selection and reporting completely end-to-end.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: N-gram keyness is purely combinatorial and statistically bounded so fully automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: N-gram length and keyness statistic are usually extractable from methods sections.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Minimum frequency and filtering rules are often but not always explicitly reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed n-gram settings and keyness thresholds yield deterministic outputs.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: #LancsBox, AntConc, kfNgram, and quanteda all support n-gram keyness extraction.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement is needed to interpret which multi-word units are meaningful.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published n-gram tables allow direct quantitative comparison of outputs.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Frequency thresholds and n-gram sizes are easy to perturb systematically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Audit cost is modest because lists and counts are directly inspectable.

task: Semantic category keyword clustering (e.g., post-hoc USAS tagging)
notable human research: Rayson, P. (2008). From key words to key semantic domains. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 13(4), 519–549.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Semantic category clustering is feasible with established tagging tools and manual review.
Research Significance — Explanation: Semantic domains add important interpretive value beyond isolated keyword forms.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can apply USAS taggers and cluster but may misclassify borderline items.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Improved context-aware models will achieve near-perfect semantic disambiguation.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: A perfect semantic tagger would make the entire task fully automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Rayson's method is well-documented and largely parseable into executable steps.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Tagset versions and manual correction rules are sometimes underreported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Automatic tagging is deterministic if models and software versions are fixed.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Wmatrix and pyMUSAS provide usable end-to-end tool support for this task.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate judgement is needed to interpret and merge semantic category boundaries.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published category rankings provide partial but useful objective ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Taggers and category thresholds can be varied systematically to test sensitivity.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Auditing requires checking semantic classifications and cluster interpretations manually.

task: Diachronic keyword comparison (keyness across two time periods)
notable human research: Baker, P. (2011). Times may change, but we will always have money: Diachronic variation in recent British English. Journal of English Linguistics, 39(1), 65–88.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Diachronic keyword comparison is feasible with time-segmented corpora and standard tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: Diachronic keyness directly supports historical linguistic and discourse interpretation.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute period comparisons reliably when corpora are available.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Within a decade this will be a fully automated one-click operation.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: With fixed periods and corpora the statistical analysis is fully automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Period definitions and keyness formulas are usually extractable from methods prose.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Corpus sampling and exact period boundaries are not always fully specified.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Identical period corpora and statistics give perfectly repeatable results.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: CQPweb and #LancsBox handle diachronic keyness directly and effectively.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Interpretation of temporal socio-historical drivers requires some contextual judgement.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published diachronic keyword tables provide measurable replication targets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Time windows and thresholds can be varied programmatically to test sensitivity.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires checking period construction as well as statistical outputs.

task: MI-based collocation with fixed window (±3 span, explicit frequency threshold)
notable human research: Church, K. W., & Hanks, P. (1990). Word association norms, mutual information, and lexicography. Computational Linguistics, 16(1), 22–29.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Fixed-window MI collocation is routine with any standard corpus concordancer.
Research Significance — Explanation: MI is foundational for establishing lexical association strength in corpora.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can script and run MI collocation analysis very reliably.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: This routine calculation will be entirely automated as a trivial pipeline step.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The fixed-window MI calculation is a purely mathematical operation fully machine-executable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The window span and frequency threshold are straightforwardly extractable from methods.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: The task explicitly supplies the key parameters (±3 span, threshold) needed for execution.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed counts and MI formula produce identical collocation rankings every time.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: AntConc, #LancsBox, Sketch Engine, and quanteda all implement window collocation.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Almost no qualitative judgement is needed to compute the MI collocate list.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published collocate tables and MI scores provide exact objective replication targets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Window size and frequency thresholds are trivially easy to vary systematically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification mainly involves count and formula spot-checks requiring minimal time.

task: Log-Dice collocation ranking (±5 span)
notable human research: Rychlý, P. (2008). A lexicographer-friendly association score. In Proceedings of RASLAN 2008 (pp. 6–9). Masaryk University.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Log-Dice collocation ranking is easy for corpus-trained humans with standard tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: Log-Dice is a key frequency-independent lexicographic ranking score widely adopted.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute Log-Dice rankings from token counts with full reliability.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI will automate Log-Dice workflows with no oversight whatsoever.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The Log-Dice formula and window counting are fully automatable without limitation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Rychlý's formula is fully formal and trivially parseable into executable code.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Frequency filters and tokenisation choices may still need explicit reporting.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed counts and formula yield deterministic bit-identical Log-Dice scores.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Sketch Engine and #LancsBox provide turnkey Log-Dice collocation support.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: The computational ranking itself needs negligible human interpretation.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published rankings and scores permit direct objective comparison.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Window spans and cut-offs can be swept trivially and easily.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Audit cost is very low because calculations are fully transparent.

task: Asymmetric window collocation (e.g., L3R1 non-symmetric span)
notable human research: Brezina, V., McEnery, T., & Wattam, S. (2015). Collocations in context: A new perspective on collocation networks. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 20(2), 139–173.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Asymmetric collocation is feasible but needs careful query setup in appropriate tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: Asymmetric windows capture directional syntactic dependencies refining collocation analysis.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can implement non-symmetric windows reliably with clear parameter specification.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should reliably manage any asymmetric window configuration and visualisation.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: No theoretical barrier prevents full automation of asymmetric collocation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Window notation such as L3R1 is readily machine-readable and unambiguous.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Studies may omit tokenisation and filtering details beyond the span itself.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Given fixed spans and corpora the output is entirely deterministic.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: #LancsBox GraphColl is purpose-built and other tools support directional scripting.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement is needed to interpret directional asymmetry findings.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published collocate rankings and networks provide objective comparison points.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Left and right spans can be varied systematically and programmatically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification mostly checks whether window logic was applied correctly.

task: Collocational profile comparison across registers
notable human research: Xiao, R., & McEnery, T. (2006). Collocation, semantic prosody, and near synonymy: A cross-linguistic perspective. Applied Linguistics, 27(1), 103–129.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Register-based collocation comparison is feasible but analytically labour-intensive.
Research Significance — Explanation: Cross-register collocational profiles are important for synonym and register studies.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute profiles but struggles with nuanced semantic prosody interpretation.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Substantial improvement expected with better contextual semantic models.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Full automation is possible if register labels and metrics are fixed though interpretation limits it.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Methods prose can identify windows and comparison metrics reasonably well.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Register definitions and corpus balancing are often incompletely reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed corpora and metrics give deterministic comparative tables.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Corpus tools support collocation by subcorpus or register effectively.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate-to-high judgement is needed to interpret register-specific semantic patterns.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published tables provide partial but not complete ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Registers and association thresholds can be varied tractably to test robustness.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Auditing requires reviewing both statistics and substantive interpretations.

task: Second-order collocation (collocation of collocates)
notable human research: Williams, G. (1998). Collocational networks: Interlocking patterns of lexis in a corpus of plant biology research articles. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 3(1), 151–171.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Second-order collocation is feasible but more complex to execute manually.
Research Significance — Explanation: Collocation networks reveal important structure beyond first-order associations.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute networks but may mishandle design choices on pruning.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should handle second-order network construction more reliably.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Graph-based lexical analysis is mathematically bounded and fully automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Published descriptions can often be parsed into network-building procedural steps.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Cut-offs and pruning rules for network inclusion are frequently underspecified.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed collocate sets and thresholds produce repeatable deterministic networks.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: #LancsBox GraphColl handles this directly; network libraries support the analysis.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement is needed for pruning decisions and interpreting network patterns.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published network diagrams offer only partial and sometimes imprecise ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Thresholds and graph parameters can be varied systematically by AI.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires checking multi-stage extraction and network construction logic.

task: DP-based dispersion analysis (Gries's Deviation of Proportions)
notable human research: Gries, S. Th. (2008). Dispersions and adjusted frequencies in corpora. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 13(4), 403–437.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: DP dispersion is straightforward for humans with formulas or standard software.
Research Significance — Explanation: Dispersion is increasingly recognised as essential alongside raw frequency.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute DP accurately and rapidly from segment counts.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI will fully automate DP extraction as a routine normalisation step.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: DP is a deterministic statistic with complete automation potential.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Gries gives an explicit formula that is trivially parseable into code.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Part-size and corpus partitioning details are mostly reported explicitly.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Identical segment counts yield identical DP values every time.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Gries's R scripts plus quanteda and other packages support DP easily.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Negligible interpretive judgement is needed to calculate the DP score.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published DP tables give direct objective replication targets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Segment sizes and dispersion metrics can be varied trivially and easily.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Auditing DP requires only count and formula spot-checks.

task: Juilland's D comparison across registers
notable human research: Juilland, A., & Chang-Rodriguez, E. (1964). Frequency dictionary of Spanish words. Mouton.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Juilland's D is trivially computable with register frequency counts.
Research Significance — Explanation: Juilland's D is useful but largely superseded by newer dispersion measures.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute Juilland's D from tabulated counts immediately.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI will make this fully routine and invisible.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The statistic is purely arithmetic and entirely automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The original formula is fully explicit and well-known.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Register partitions and normalisation details may occasionally be missing.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed register counts yield identical Juilland's D values deterministically.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: General statistical tools and dispersion R packages compute the measure readily.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Very little interpretive judgement is required once registers are defined.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published D values enable direct objective verification checks.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Alternative partitions and measures can be tested systematically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification cost is very low for a formula-based statistic.

task: Frequency vs. dispersion trade-off mapping
notable human research: Gries, S. Th. (2020). Analyzing dispersion. In M. Paquot & S. Th. Gries (Eds.), A practical handbook of corpus linguistics. Springer.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Frequency-dispersion mapping is feasible with standard spreadsheet or scripting tools.
Research Significance — Explanation: The trade-off is important for robust corpus frequency interpretation and methodology.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute and plot the mapping with appropriate guidance.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should automate exploratory dispersion diagnostics well and completely.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The mapping is fully automatable once metrics and visualisation choices are selected.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Methods prose may describe the plot but omit exact display and binning choices.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Metric choices and binning rules are often only partly explicit in publications.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed metrics and data produce identical coordinate mappings deterministically.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: R and Python provide mature plotting and dispersion tooling for this task.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement is needed to interpret outlier patterns and cluster boundaries.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published figures and tables provide measurable but imperfect replication targets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Metrics bins and thresholds can be varied systematically by AI.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires checking both calculations and visual encoding choices.

task: CQL/CQP concordance search with explicit query string
notable human research: Evert, S., & Hardie, A. (2011). Twenty-first century Corpus Workbench: Updating a query architecture for the new millennium. In Proceedings of the Corpus Linguistics 2011 Conference. University of Birmingham.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Running an explicit CQL query is trivial for trained corpus linguistics users.
Research Significance — Explanation: CQL concordancing underpins the majority of corpus grammar and pattern studies.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can perfectly execute explicit CQL queries against corpus databases.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI will natively translate natural language into flawless complex CQL queries.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Executing a formal deterministic search query is fundamentally a machine task.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: An explicit query string is by definition a machine-executable specification.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Published CQL queries inherently contain exactly all needed parameters.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Identical indexed corpora and queries return identical hit lists every time.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Corpus Workbench CQPweb and Sketch Engine are mature and purpose-built.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Running a provided query string requires absolutely zero interpretive judgement.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Hit counts and concordance lines provide clear unambiguous ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Query variants can be tested but may change linguistic scope and meaning.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification is essentially instantaneous by checking hit count and sample lines.

task: Lexico-grammatical pattern extraction via dependency parsing (SVO triples)
notable human research: Mason, O., & Hunston, S. (2004). The automatic recognition of verb patterns: A feasibility study. International Journal of Corpus Linguistics, 9(2), 253–270.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: SVO extraction is feasible but parser errors require manual review and correction.
Research Significance — Explanation: Lexico-grammatical patterns are important for corpus grammar and pattern research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI with spaCy or Stanza can parse and extract SVO triples with good accuracy.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future deep learning parsers will substantially reduce extraction errors.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Near-perfect parsing would make SVO extraction largely fully automatable.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Methods prose can often specify dependencies and triple rules reasonably clearly.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Parser versions and specific dependency rule details are often incompletely reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Determinism depends on fixed parser models and preprocessing pipeline settings.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: spaCy Stanza and UDPipe provide very robust mature dependency parsing pipelines.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Moderate judgement is needed to validate extracted grammatical relations.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Gold-parsed treebanks and published examples provide limited but useful ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Parser and confidence thresholds can be varied systematically across models.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Auditing requires manual inspection of sampled parsed triples which takes moderate time.

task: Constructional frequency profiling (syntactic construction counts across genres)
notable human research: Stefanowitsch, A. (2013). Collostructional analysis. In T. Hoffmann & G. Trousdale (Eds.), The Oxford handbook of construction grammar. Oxford University Press.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Constructional profiling is feasible but annotation-intensive and requires expert linguistic knowledge.
Research Significance — Explanation: Construction frequencies are central to usage-based grammar and genre comparison research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can approximate counts but construction operationalisation remains difficult.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Major progress expected with better parsers and semantic-syntactic models.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: With formally defined constructions the task could be highly automatable in principle.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Constructional definitions in prose often resist clean translation into executable queries.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Construction boundaries and genre sampling criteria are often theoretically debated and vague.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Outputs are deterministic only if parsing rules and construction definitions are fully fixed.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Parsing tools help but construction-specific pipelines are uneven across languages and genres.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Substantial theoretical judgement is needed to define and validate what counts as a construction.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published counts provide partial rather than exhaustive ground truth for comparison.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Definitions and parser settings can be varied but require careful theoretical justification.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires expert linguistic review of construction matches which is time-consuming.

task: Semantic prosody assessment via concordance sampling
notable human research: Louw, B. (1993). Irony in the text or insincerity in the writer? The diagnostic potential of semantic prosodies. In M. Baker, G. Francis, & E. Tognini-Bonelli (Eds.), Text and technology (pp. 157–176). John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Semantic prosody assessment is feasible but requires extensive close reading and interpretation.
Research Significance — Explanation: Semantic prosody is a major theoretical concept in corpus-assisted discourse studies.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can summarise concordances but cannot reliably replace expert prosody judgement.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Context-aware AI will increasingly detect evaluative aura but may still need oversight.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Inherent subjectivity and context-dependence caps theoretical full replaceability.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Methods prose rarely yields a fully executable prosody specification or criteria.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Sampling sizes and evaluative classification criteria are often only loosely reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Qualitative coding varies with annotator making outputs not fully deterministic.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Concordancers exist but prosody interpretation lacks end-to-end automated tools.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Semantic prosody requires heavy contextual cultural and evaluative human judgement.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published interpretations provide weak and subjective objective ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Some sensitivity analysis is possible through sampling and window variation.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Auditing AI prosody claims requires extensive expert human concordance review.

task: Discourse preference profiling (co-occurrence of evaluative lexis around a target)
notable human research: Partington, A., Duguid, A., & Taylor, C. (2013). Patterns and meanings in discourse: Theory and practice in corpus-assisted discourse studies (CADS). John Benjamins.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Discourse preference profiling is feasible but analytically demanding and slow.
Research Significance — Explanation: It is important for linking lexical patterns to discourse meanings and ideology.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can count co-occurrences but struggles with discourse interpretation nuances.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should improve evaluative lexicon construction and discourse summarisation.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Even ideal systems must model contextual discourse meaning limiting full automation.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Published prose is hard to convert into exact evaluative categories and criteria.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Lexicons windows and coding criteria are often underreported in publications.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Counts are deterministic but evaluative category assignment may vary.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Tools support co-occurrence counting but not complete CADS interpretation.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: High contextual and theoretical judgement is needed for evaluative discourse claims.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published findings provide partial rather than exact ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Windows and lexicons can be varied to test sensitivity of discourse findings.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires reviewing both lexical counts and their discourse interpretations.

task: Overuse/underuse analysis in learner corpus (%DIFF or odds ratio vs. native reference)
notable human research: Granger, S. (1998). The computer learner corpus: A versatile new source of data for SLA research. In S. Granger (Ed.), Learner English on computer (pp. 3–18). Addison Wesley Longman.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Learner overuse/underuse analysis is routine with aligned learner and native corpora.
Research Significance — Explanation: Overuse and underuse measures are foundational in learner corpus research methodology.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute %DIFF or odds ratios reliably and quickly.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should automate corpus matching normalisation and reporting completely.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The comparative statistics are purely arithmetic and fully automatable with fixed inputs.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: The statistic and reference corpus specification are usually clearly extractable.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Learner grouping and normalisation details are often reported with sufficient explicitness.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed counts and formulas yield deterministic results every time.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Learner corpus tools and statistical packages support the workflow well.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Some judgement is needed to interpret pedagogical relevance of overuse patterns.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Published frequency and effect-size tables provide objective replication targets.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Reference corpus choices and thresholds can be varied systematically.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification mainly checks corpus matching and formula application requiring minimal time.

task: Error-tagged frequency profiling (tagged error categories across L1 groups)
notable human research: Dagneaux, E., Denness, S., & Granger, S. (1998). Computer-aided error analysis. System, 26(2), 163–174.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Error-tag frequency profiling is feasible when pre-existing tags are available but tagging is laborious.
Research Significance — Explanation: Error profiles are useful for learner corpus diagnosis and SLA research.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can tabulate existing error tags reliably but cannot create perfect tags autonomously.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI trained on learner data should substantially improve error tagging accuracy.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: Given validated tags the profiling step is fully automatable in principle.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Methods can usually specify tag categories and L1 grouping structure.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: Error taxonomy details and annotation reliability criteria are sometimes underreported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Counting existing tags yields deterministic category counts; initial tagging is not deterministic.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: Learner corpus tools support tagged frequency profiling but tagging pipelines are limited.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Substantial linguistic judgement remains needed in initial error identification and categorisation.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Tagged corpora and published category tables provide useful but imperfect ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Groups and category aggregations can be varied systematically once tags exist.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification requires checking both tag meanings and frequency calculations with expertise.

task: Burrows's Delta authorship attribution (most-frequent-word profiles)
notable human research: Burrows, J. (2002). 'Delta': A measure of stylistic difference and a guide to likely authorship. Literary and Linguistic Computing, 17(3), 267–287.
Human Feasibility — Explanation: Burrows's Delta is straightforward to compute with stylometric software like stylo.
Research Significance — Explanation: Delta is a foundational highly influential technique in authorship attribution and stylometry.
Current AI Replaceability — Explanation: Current AI can compute Delta and rank authorship candidates with full reliability.
AI Replaceability in 10 Years — Explanat: Future AI should automate robust stylometric pipelines completely without oversight.
Theoretical Maximum AI Replaceability —: The Delta calculation is purely mathematical and fully automatable with fixed preprocessing.
Specification Extractability — Explanati: Burrows's procedure is fully formal with explicit z-score transformations documented.
Parameter Explicitness — Explanation: MFW count feature standardisation and distance metric settings are usually reported.
Execution Determinism — Explanation: Fixed corpora and parameters yield identical Delta scores deterministically every time.
Tool-chain Maturity — Explanation: The stylo R package is purpose-built and provides mature Delta implementation.
Interpretive Judgement Load — Explanatio: Limited judgement is needed beyond corpus preparation and final attribution interpretation.
Evaluation / Ground-Truth Availability —: Known authorship labels provide strong objective evaluation and ground truth.
Robustness Tractability — Explanation: Feature counts MFW ranges and normalisation settings can be swept trivially.
Verification Cost — Explanation: Verification mainly checks preprocessing and score reproduction requiring minimal time.
